timeline.event: Born in Denmark
Died of scurvy on Bering Island

keyAchievements.title: Explored and named the Bering Strait
keyAchievements.description: Commissioned by Peter the Great of Russia, he explored the strait between the continents of Asia and America, naming it the Bering Strait.

keyAchievements.title: Explored the North Pacific region
keyAchievements.description: He explored the Kamchatka Peninsula, the Commander Islands, the Aleutian Islands, and Alaska.

keyAchievements.title: Origin of place names
keyAchievements.description: Place names such as the Bering Strait, the Bering Sea, and Bering Island were named after Vitus Bering.

faq.question: Which country was Vitus Bering from?
faq.answer: He was a navigator and explorer born in Denmark.

faq.question: What did Vitus Bering explore?
faq.answer: Commissioned by Peter the Great of Russia, he explored the strait between the continents of Asia and America, and also explored the Kamchatka Peninsula, the Commander Islands, the Aleutian Islands, and Alaska.

faq.question: How did Vitus Bering die?
faq.answer: He died of scurvy on Bering Island in 1741.

faq.question: What place names are named after Vitus Bering?
faq.answer: The Bering Strait, the Bering Sea, and Bering Island, among others.

timeline.event: Born near Lisbon
Departed on an expedition to the west coast of Africa with three ships by order of John II to discover Ethiopia
Reached the southern tip of Africa with two ships
Rounded the Cape of Good Hope due to a storm late that year but turned back due to mutinous sentiments among the crew
Discovered Cape Point on his return voyage and named it 'Cape of Storms'
Arrived at the Cape Verde Islands following Vasco da Gama
Perished in a storm while sailing to Brazil with Pedro Álvares Cabral

keyAchievements.title: Reached the southern tip of Africa
keyAchievements.description: In 1488, he reached the southern tip of Africa with two ships, laying the groundwork for the discovery of the sea route to India.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Cape of Storms
keyAchievements.description: On his return voyage, he discovered Cape Point and named it the 'Cape of Storms', which was later changed to the 'Cape of Good Hope' by John II.

faq.question: When was Bartolomeu Dias born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Born around 1450 near Lisbon, he died on May 29, 1500, shipwrecked in a storm during a voyage to Brazil.

faq.question: What are Bartolomeu Dias's main achievements?
faq.answer: In 1488, he reached the southern tip of Africa, laying the groundwork for the discovery of the sea route to India, and on his return voyage, he discovered Cape Point and named it the 'Cape of Storms'.

faq.question: Who named the 'Cape of Good Hope'?
faq.answer: John II renamed the 'Cape of Storms', discovered by Bartolomeu Dias, to the 'Cape of Good Hope'.

timeline.event: Born the son of a vicar in Berkeley, Gloucestershire.
Became an apprentice to surgeon Daniel Ludlow in Sodbury.
Began studying medicine under John Hunter at St. George's Hospital, University of London.
Became a general practitioner in his hometown.
Developed an interest in smallpox.
Collected his observations on smallpox.
Married Catherine Kingscote.
Received a Doctor of Medicine degree from the University of St Andrews in Scotland.
John Hunter died, ending their friendship.
Successfully performed the first vaccination on a local boy.
Reported the results of 23 experiments to the Royal Society and published his paper 'An Inquiry into the Causes and Effects of the Variolae Vaccinae'.
Received a grant (part of a total of £30,000) from the government.
The Royal Jennerian Society was established in London to promote vaccination.
Received the remaining grant (of the total £30,000) from the government.
Made his last visit to London.
Died in his hometown of a stroke.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the cowpox method
keyAchievements.description: He discovered the cowpox method, earning him the title 'Father of Immunology'.

keyAchievements.title: Coined the term 'vaccine'
keyAchievements.description: He created the terms 'vaccine' and 'vaccination' from the Latin word 'vacca', meaning cow.

keyAchievements.title: Development and dissemination of the smallpox vaccine
keyAchievements.description: On May 14, 1796, he successfully performed the first cowpox inoculation on a boy, contributing to the development and global dissemination of the smallpox vaccine.

faq.question: Who was Edward Jenner?
faq.answer: Edward Jenner was a British physician, the discoverer of the cowpox method, and the 'Father of Immunology'.

faq.question: When was Edward Jenner born and when did he die?
faq.answer: Edward Jenner was born on May 17, 1749, and died on January 26, 1823.

faq.question: What was the occasion for Edward Jenner coining the term 'vaccine'?
faq.answer: Jenner coined the terms 'vaccine' and 'vaccination' from the Latin word 'vacca', meaning cow.

faq.question: What are Edward Jenner's main achievements?
faq.answer: Edward Jenner discovered the cowpox method and successfully performed the first cowpox inoculation on a boy on May 14, 1796, developing the smallpox vaccine.

timeline.event: December 10, Emily Elizabeth Dickinson born in Amherst, Massachusetts, USA
May 15, Died at the age of fifty-five

keyAchievements.title: Poetry creation
keyAchievements.description: She created nearly 2000 poems.

keyAchievements.title: Main themes of poetry
keyAchievements.description: She created poems with love, death, separation, soul, and heaven as main themes.

keyAchievements.title: Public recognition during her lifetime
keyAchievements.description: She was not publicly recognized during her lifetime, and only four of her poems were published in anthologies.

keyAchievements.title: Literary exchanges during her lifetime
keyAchievements.description: She consistently exchanged poems through correspondence with a few poets and intellectuals who recognized her talent during her lifetime.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous recognition of genius
keyAchievements.description: After her death, her poetry collection was published by her younger sister Lavinia Norcross Dickinson, and her genius was widely recognized.

keyAchievements.title: Musical reinterpretation of her poetry
keyAchievements.description: Her poems were set to music as vocal pieces by renowned composers such as Aaron Copland.

faq.question: Was Emily Dickinson recognized by the public during her lifetime?
faq.answer: She was not publicly recognized during her lifetime, and only four of her poems were published in anthologies.

faq.question: What themes did Emily Dickinson's poetry primarily deal with?
faq.answer: She created poems with love, death, separation, soul, and heaven as main themes.

faq.question: How many poems did Emily Dickinson write?
faq.answer: She wrote nearly 2000 poems.

faq.question: Was Emily Dickinson a recluse poet?
faq.answer: She is known as a recluse poet who severely avoided going out, but as a child, she had a lively personality, going out to the fields every day and playing with neighborhood children.

timeline.event: Born in Fuendetodos, Aragon, Spain.
Goya's family acquired a house in Zaragoza and moved there a few years later.
Submitted an application for admission to the Royal Academy of Fine Arts but was rejected.
Was moved by the works of Velázquez and Rembrandt in Madrid.
Submitted an application for admission to the Royal Academy of Fine Arts but was rejected.
Traveled to Italy, won second prize in the Parma painting exhibition, and returned to Zaragoza later that year to become a painter for the Basilica of Our Lady of the Pillar.
Married Josefa, sister of Bayeu.
Painted a portrait of Count Floridablanca, a close associate of Charles III.
Began painting a portrait of Charles III.
Charles III died.
Charles IV ascended to the throne, and Goya became a court painter.
Contracted cholera and lost his hearing due to high fever.
Painted a series of fantastic pictures.
Painted frescoes in the Royal Chapel of St. Anthony of La Florida in Madrid.
Published the print series 'Los Caprichos' and became first court painter.
Painted 'The Family of Charles IV' and 'The Naked Maja'.
Painted 'The Clothed Maja'.
The Peninsular War broke out between France and Spain, and Goya created documentary paintings about it.
Produced the print series 'The Disasters of War'.
His wife Josefa died.
The Spanish Inquisition confiscated the Maja series as obscene.
French forces withdrew, and Ferdinand VII returned to the throne.
Moved to Bordeaux, France.
Returned to Spain but went back to Bordeaux due to deteriorating health.
Died at the age of 82.

keyAchievements.title: Status as a Romantic painter and printmaker
keyAchievements.description: Known as a representative Romantic painter and printmaker of Spain, and a genius artist who represented 18th-century Spanish painting and foreshadowed Impressionism.

keyAchievements.title: Court painter activities
keyAchievements.description: Became a court painter in 1789 and first court painter in 1799, painting portraits of many royals and aristocrats, including the king and queen.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of the print series 'Los Caprichos'
keyAchievements.description: In 1799, he published 'Los Caprichos', a series of prints created using aquatint, a type of etching, exposing the corruption of the Catholic Church and superstitions.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of war documentary paintings and print series
keyAchievements.description: He left documentary paintings about the Peninsular War, 'The Second of May 1808' and 'The Third of May 1808', and produced the print series 'The Disasters of War', depicting the horrors of war.

keyAchievements.title: The 'Maja' series
keyAchievements.description: He painted 'The Naked Maja' and 'The Clothed Maja', with 'The Naked Maja' being regarded as 'the first life-size female nude in Western art'.

keyAchievements.title: The 'Black Paintings' series
keyAchievements.description: In his later years, at the 'House of the Deaf Man', he created the 'Black Paintings' series, depicting madness hidden behind reason, among which 'Saturn Devouring His Son' is famous.

faq.question: How is Francisco Goya evaluated as a painter?
faq.answer: He is regarded as a representative Romantic painter and printmaker of Spain, and a genius artist who represented 18th-century Spanish painting and foreshadowed Impressionism.

faq.question: When did Goya lose his hearing?
faq.answer: He lost his hearing in 1792 due to a high fever after contracting cholera.

faq.question: What are Goya's representative print series?
faq.answer: 'Los Caprichos', published in 1799, and 'The Disasters of War', produced from 1810 to 1820.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of Goya's 'Maja' series?
faq.answer: 'The Naked Maja' and 'The Clothed Maja' are works depicting the same woman in the same pose, and 'The Naked Maja' is considered 'the first life-size female nude in Western art'.

faq.question: What was the trend of Goya's later works?
faq.answer: After losing his hearing in 1792, his paintings became darker. After the Peninsular War, he painted works with heavy themes and dark tones, and in his later years, he created the 'Black Paintings' series at the 'House of the Deaf Man'.

timeline.event: Mary Ann Evans (George Eliot) born
Family moved to Griff House
Mother died
Completed English translation of Strauss's 'The Life of Jesus'
Father died
Traveled to Switzerland with the Bray family
Returned to England and moved to London
Became assistant editor of 'The Westminster Review'
Began contributing essays and commentaries to 'The Westminster Review'
Translated Feuerbach's 'The Essence of Christianity'
Left 'The Westminster Review'
Published the novel 'Adam Bede'
Published the novel 'The Mill on the Floss'
Published the novel 'Silas Marner'
Published the novel 'Romola'
Published the novel 'Middlemarch'
Published the novel 'Daniel Deronda'
George Eliot died

keyAchievements.title: Important writer of the Victorian era
keyAchievements.description: Active as a novelist, poet, journalist, and translator, she was one of the most important writers of the Victorian era.

keyAchievements.title: Authored major novels
keyAchievements.description: She wrote a total of seven novels, including 'Adam Bede', 'The Mill on the Floss', 'Silas Marner', 'Romola', 'Middlemarch', and 'Daniel Deronda', showcasing realism and psychological insight.

keyAchievements.title: Translated philosophical works
keyAchievements.description: She translated Strauss's 'The Life of Jesus' (1846) and Feuerbach's 'The Essence of Christianity' (1854).

keyAchievements.title: Editorial work at 'The Westminster Review'
keyAchievements.description: From 1851 to mid-1854, she worked as an assistant editor for 'The Westminster Review', carrying out substantial publishing work and contributing many essays and commentaries.

keyAchievements.title: Overcoming prejudice through pen name
keyAchievements.description: She used the pen name 'George Eliot' to overcome prejudice against female writers and to ensure her works were evaluated objectively.

faq.question: What was George Eliot's real name?
faq.answer: George Eliot's real name was Mary Ann Evans.

faq.question: What was the main reason George Eliot used a pen name?
faq.answer: She wished to escape the prejudice that female authors could only write frivolous romance novels, to separate her novels from her identity as an editor and critic, and to avoid personal scandal.

faq.question: What are George Eliot's representative novels?
faq.answer: 'Adam Bede', 'The Mill on the Floss', 'Silas Marner', 'Romola', 'Middlemarch', and 'Daniel Deronda' are among them.

faq.question: What are the characteristics of her novels?
faq.answer: Most of her novels are set in rural England and are famous for their realism and psychological insight.

faq.question: What major works did George Eliot translate?
faq.answer: She translated Strauss's 'The Life of Jesus' and Feuerbach's 'The Essence of Christianity'.

timeline.event: Most historians believe he was born in England.
Departed from Gravesend aboard the Hopewell to begin his first significant voyage.
The Hopewell and her crew returned to England.
Departed again aboard the Hopewell to seek the Northeast Passage.
Sailed aboard the Halve Maen, commissioned by the Dutch East India Company, to find the Northeast Passage.
Set sail on the Discovery to find the Northwest Passage.
Disappeared in Hudson Bay after being abandoned in a small boat with his son John and seven companions during a mutiny.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of the Hudson River, Hudson Strait, and Hudson Bay
keyAchievements.description: In 1610, he explored northern Canada and discovered the Hudson River, Hudson Strait, and Hudson Bay.

keyAchievements.title: Discovery of North American waterways
keyAchievements.description: Although he failed to find a passage to Asia, he discovered various waterways in North America.

keyAchievements.title: Laid the groundwork for colonization and trade
keyAchievements.description: His discoveries laid the groundwork for further European voyages for colonization and trade in North America.

faq.question: When was Henry Hudson born?
faq.answer: Most historians believe Henry Hudson was born in England around 1565.

faq.question: How did Henry Hudson disappear?
faq.answer: On June 23, 1611, he disappeared after being forcibly transferred to a small boat with his son John and seven companions and abandoned in Hudson Bay due to a crew mutiny.

faq.question: What was Henry Hudson's main purpose for exploration?
faq.answer: Henry Hudson's main purpose for exploration was to find a Northwest or Northeast Passage to Asia.

faq.question: What is Henry Hudson's legacy of exploration?
faq.answer: His discoveries laid the groundwork for the colonization and trade of North America, and many of the areas he explored and sailed through are named after him today.

timeline.event: Homer born
Wrote 'The Iliad' and 'The Odyssey' (estimated)

keyAchievements.title: Authored 'The Iliad' and 'The Odyssey'
keyAchievements.description: He is known as the author of 'The Iliad' and 'The Odyssey', two epic poems that form the foundation of ancient Greek literature.

faq.question: Who was Homer?
faq.answer: Homer was an ancient Greek poet born around the 8th century BCE, known as the author of 'The Iliad' and 'The Odyssey'.

faq.question: What are Homer's major works?
faq.answer: Homer is known as the author of 'The Iliad' and 'The Odyssey', two epic poems that form the foundation of ancient Greek literature.

faq.question: What is known about Homer's life?
faq.answer: In ancient times, a widespread belief was that he was a blind bard from Ionia, but modern scholars consider this a legend. Little concrete information about his life is known.

faq.question: When were 'The Iliad' and 'The Odyssey' written?
faq.answer: These poems are believed to have been composed around the late 8th or early 7th century BCE.

timeline.event: Born in Steventon Rectory, Hampshire.
Educated at Abbey House School for Girls in Reading, Berkshire.
Began writing novels at age 14.
Wrote 'Elinor and Marianne' in epistolary form.
Met a young Irishman named Tom Lefroy, and wrote 'First Impressions'.
Began writing 'Sense and Sensibility', based on 'Elinor and Marianne'.
Her father resigned his rectorship, and the family moved to Bath (Somerset).
Received a marriage proposal from Harris Bigg-Wither but rejected it after one day.
Sold 'Lady Susan' to Crossed & Co.
Her father died.
Settled in a caretaker's house in Chawton with the help of her third brother Edward.
Submitted 'Mansfield Park' and published 'Sense and Sensibility' anonymously.
Published 'Pride and Prejudice', based on 'First Impressions'.
Published 'Mansfield Park'.
Entertained the Prince Regent (later George IV) and dedicated 'Emma' just before its publication.
Her health frequently deteriorated, and she was often confined to bed.
Died in Winchester at the age of 42 during the writing of 'Sanditon'.
'Northanger Abbey' and 'Persuasion' were published.

keyAchievements.title: Began writing novels
keyAchievements.description: She began writing novels at age 14 and grew into a novelist with the support of her family.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Sense and Sensibility'
keyAchievements.description: One of her first works, published anonymously in 1811.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of 'Pride and Prejudice'
keyAchievements.description: Published in January 1813; the original title was 'First Impressions'.

keyAchievements.title: Publication and dedication of 'Emma'
keyAchievements.description: Published in October 1815 after being dedicated to the Prince Regent (later George IV).

keyAchievements.title: Founded the 'Great Tradition' of English novel
keyAchievements.description: With her delicate eye and witty prose, she portrayed the lives of middle and upper-class English women in the 18th century, earning her the reputation of founding the 'Great Tradition' of the English novel.

keyAchievements.title: Socially critical realism literature
keyAchievements.description: With keen observation and a sharp critical perspective, she satirized the materialistic trends and hypocrisy of her contemporary society, creating a sophisticated world of realism.

keyAchievements.title: Posthumous literary re-evaluation and popular culture influence
keyAchievements.description: From the late 19th century, her works gained critical acclaim and entered the literary canon. In the late 20th century, her works were adapted into films and plays, establishing them as a popular cult.

faq.question: When was Jane Austen born?
faq.answer: She was born on December 16, 1775, in Steventon Rectory, Hampshire.

faq.question: What are Jane Austen's representative works?
faq.answer: 'Sense and Sensibility' (1811), 'Pride and Prejudice' (1813), 'Mansfield Park' (1814), and 'Emma' (1816) are among them.

faq.question: What kind of novels did Jane Austen write?
faq.answer: Her novels are characterized by a delicate eye and witty prose, dealing with the lives of middle and upper-class English women in the 18th century.

faq.question: Was Jane Austen famous during her lifetime?
faq.answer: She was not very famous during her lifetime, but her works gained popularity in the 20th century, with several film adaptations.

faq.question: Was Jane Austen married?
faq.answer: She had a romance with a young man named Tom Lefroy that did not come to fruition, and in 1802, she received a marriage proposal from Harris Bigg-Wither but rejected it after one day.

faq.question: When did Jane Austen die?
faq.answer: She died on July 18, 1817, at the age of 42.

timeline.event: Born in Pentonville, London, England
Began learning Greek
Began learning Latin, Euclid, and algebra, and tutored his younger siblings
Reached a level where he could read original texts of Plato and Demosthenes
Began studying scholastic logic and original texts of Aristotle's logic
Began studying political economy
Spent a year in France, attending lectures on chemistry, zoology, and logic at the University of Montpellier
Suffered a nervous breakdown
Founded the Utilitarian Society with friends
Published 'A System of Logic'
Published 'Essays on Some Unsettled Questions of Political Economy'
Published 'Principles of Political Economy'
Married Harriet Taylor
Harriet Taylor died and resigned from the East India Company
Published 'On Liberty'
Published 'Utilitarianism' and 'Considerations on Representative Government'
Appointed Rector of St Andrews University and elected Member of Parliament for Westminster, London
End of term as Member of Parliament
First to advocate for women's suffrage in Parliament and published 'The Subjection of Women'
Died in Avignon, France, and published 'Autobiography'
Published 'Three Essays on Religion'
Published 'Socialism'

keyAchievements.title: Completion of a System of Logic
keyAchievements.description: In 1843, he published 'A System of Logic', completing Bacon's inductive logic and proposing groundbreaking methodology for natural science research.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of liberal political theory
keyAchievements.description: Through 'On Liberty', he left a classic treatise on the concept of liberty, advocating freedom of expression as a fundamental civil right and presenting the core principles of modern liberal democracy.

keyAchievements.title: Development of utilitarianism
keyAchievements.description: He developed his own system by adding Saint-Simonianism and Romanticism to the utilitarianism of his godfather and mentor, Bentham.

keyAchievements.title: Advocacy for women's rights and social reform
keyAchievements.description: As a Member of Parliament, he advocated for various social reforms including women's rights, proportional representation, trade unions, and cooperative organization of agricultural land, and was the first in Parliament to argue for women's suffrage.

keyAchievements.title: Pioneer of market socialist theory
keyAchievements.description: He argued for replacing capitalist enterprises with worker cooperatives, earning him recognition as one of the early market socialist theorists.

faq.question: How was John Stuart Mill's education structured?
faq.answer: He was primarily educated by his father, James Mill, and was a precocious child, learning Greek at age three and Latin and advanced mathematics at age eight.

faq.question: What were Mill's views on religion?
faq.answer: He was critical of religion itself, never had faith, rejected Christian doctrine, and considered religion the greatest enemy of liberty.

faq.question: How did Harriet Taylor influence John Stuart Mill?
faq.answer: Harriet significantly influenced Mill's thought, particularly strengthening his stance on women's rights, and impacting the writing of 'On Liberty' and 'The Subjection of Women'.

faq.question: What did John Stuart Mill think about the 'stationary state'?
faq.answer: He believed that a stationary state of capital and wealth was not terrible but could even be a far more advanced and happy state than the present, envisioning humanity being less concerned with money and more with values like dignity, nobility, and justice.

faq.question: Was John Stuart Mill a socialist?
faq.answer: He called himself a socialist and advocated for replacing capitalist enterprises with worker cooperatives, leading him to be considered one of the early market socialist theorists.
